raw_case:
Scrotal cellular angiofibroma: A case report and review of the literature
Scrotal cellular angiofibroma (CAF) is a rare, benign, soft-tissue paratesticular tumor that has been sporadically reported previously. We present a case of a 55-year old male with a scrotal mass ultimately diagnosed with scrotal CAF. Immunohistochemical analysis stained positively for desmin, CD34, and estrogen receptor. Our series is one of the only published to date demonstrating desmin-positive paratesticular CAF. Diagnosis of CAF remains difficult due to the sparsity of paratesticular CAF, its similar characteristics to spindle cell lipoma (SCL), and variability in immunohistochemical reporting.

Highlights

•Scrotal Cellular Angiofibroma is a sparsely reported paratesticular tumor.•Our case demonstrates Estrogen Receptor, CD34, and Desmin positivity on immunohistochemical analysis.•Further research is warranted to investigate paratesticular Cellular Angiofibroma pathogenesis.1

Background

Paratesticular tumors are a subset of scrotal tumors originating from mesenchymal tissue, connective tissue, or lymphoid tissue.1 They may derive from the tunica vaginalis, epididymis, spermatic cord, or local supportive tissue.2 Paratesticular tumors are rare, accounting for approximately 10 % of total scrotal tumors; however, approximately 30 % of these tumors are malignant.1,3 Sarcomatous tumors account for the majority of malignant paratesticular tumors.4 Men in their sixth decade of life are at highest risk for paratesticular tumors.2The clinical presentation of paratesticular tumors has been previously described. Typical presentation includes a scrotal mass, which is usually painless.1 The differential diagnosis for paratesticular tumors includes liposarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, angiomyofibroblastoma, and aggressive angiomyxoma.5,6 Additionally, inguinal hernia, hydrocele, spermatocele, cyst, or infection may be considered.1 After a physical exam, ultrasonography is often utilized to better characterize a paratesticular mass.1 The management of paratesticular tumors includes tumor resection and in some instances, radical inguinal orchiectomy.3,4Cellular angiofibroma (CAF) is a rare, mesenchymal-derived, soft tissue benign neoplasm.7 Typically, CAF presents within the vulvovaginal region of women in the fifth decade of life.8 CAF has been sporadically reported in the inguinoscrotal region of men, with men in their seventh decade of life being at highest risk for paratesticular CAF.9 We present a case of a scrotal CAF in a 55-year old male patient and highlight the aberrant histopathological findings.2

Case presentation

A 55-year old male presented to clinic with scrotal pain of two months duration. Pain was intermittent in nature. There were no significant exacerbating or relieving factors. His medical history was significant for hypertension, migraines, neuropathy, nephrolithiasis, erectile dysfunction, and depression. His surgical history was significant for gastric bypass, cholecystectomy, and a perforated ulcer repair. He was a non-smoker and had no history of alcohol use. He did not have a family history of any genitourinary malignancies.Upon examination, he was noted to have an abnormal mass within the midline of the scrotum. Both testicles were separate from the mass and palpably normal. There was no concern for a large hydrocele or varicocele. Overlying skin was within normal limits. Further diagnostic study with imaging was then obtained.3

Imaging

The patient underwent scrotal ultrasound which was significant for a heterogenous and hypoechoic mass visualized within the midline measuring 4.8cm. There was marked vascularity on color Doppler (Fig. 1). The testicles appeared visually normal with no evidence of abnormal testicular masses.Given these findings, Computed Tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast was obtained, which was significant for a midline scrotal mass measuring 3.6cm with marked vascularity (Fig. 2). There was no significant lymphadenopathy, bone lesions, or any other concerns for metastatic disease.4

Treatment

The patient was counseled on options and elected for surgical extirpation. A skin incision was made over the inferior scrotum in the midline over the palpable mass. The mass was then carefully dissected away from the surrounding tissue using both blunt and sharp dissection with cautery. The mass was freed away from the scrotal contents with a large margin of tissue circumferentially. The mass had a rubbery texture. Hemostasis was obtained and the scrotum was then closed in multiple layers.5

Histopathology

On pathologic evaluation, there were spindle cells and hyalinized vessels with lymphocytic infiltration (Fig. 3). In addition, on high power stain, analysis revealed wispy collagen, mast cells, and fibrous stoma, which are characteristic of CAF.10 Notably, spindle cell component of tissue excision were positive for CD34, desmin, and estrogen receptor (ER). CD31, WT1, and ERG stains were also positive in tissue sample endothelial cells. Calretinin staining was negative. Margins were free of neoplasm. Final diagnosis was CAF.6

Discussion

CAF is a benign, soft tissue tumor that was first characterized in 1997 by Nucci et al.6 Their morphologic description of CAF in the vulvovaginal and perineal region entailed a small, well-circumscribed, hyalinized vessel tumor containing uniform spindle stromal cells.6 Their immunohistochemistry analysis showed positive vimentin but negative CD34, desmin, S100, actin, and epithelial membrane antigen.6 Since Nucci et al.‘s original description of vulvovaginal CAF, sporadic case reports of scrotal CAF have been published.7,10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15In 1998, Laskin et al. reported eight cases of an “angiofibroblastoma-like-tumor” in the inguinoscrotal region.14 In comparison to Nucci et al.‘s report, Laskin et al. found collagenous rather than spindled stromal cells.14 Laskin et al. also noted increased variability of desmin in CAF located within the inguinoscrotal region.14Iwasa et al. further described CAF.9 Compared to previous CAF literature, they found repeated CD34 positivity in vulvovaginal and inguinoscrotal regions, however, desmin positivity was rare.9 Iwasa et al. also noted the presence of mast cells were correlated with CAF, as well as the association of desmin with angiomyofibroblastoma.9 Finally, they observed a larger median CAF tumor size in men (7cm) compared to women (2.8cm).9CAF typically demonstrates spindle-shaped cells, wispy collagen, myxoid stroma, and small-to- medium sized hyalinized vessels.9 CAF has a well-defined capsular membrane, rubbery texture, and is typically white or yellow in appearance.9 Our case of paratesticular CAF demonstrated wispy collagen, adipocytes, lymphocytic infiltrate, and mast cells, which are suggestive for CAF.10 Finally, the absence of tumor-suppressor RB1 gene is correlated with CAF and may aid in its distinction from other soft-tissue tumors.16 However, contrary to previous literature, our case of paratesticular CAF demonstrated positivity for desmin, which is reportedly rare. CD34 and ER positivity have been more frequently reported in CAF characterization.10 Estrogen is hypothesized to contribute to cellular angiofibroma pathogenesis, yet its precise mechanism remains unclear.15 CD34 is expressed in a variety of benign and malignant soft-tissue tumors, and therefore does not provide definitive diagnosis for CAF.17There is conflicting data regarding desmin staining for CAF in both male and female patients. Prior reports of CAF in both male and female patients are suggestive for negative desmin staining in CAF,7,12,13,16,18 but there are few reported series with positive desmin staining.11,14 Desmin is a structural component of both skeletal and smooth muscle and is used to differentiate soft-tissue tumors.17 Traditionally, its presence in immunohistochemistry staining may be suggestive of myogenic tumors such as rhabdomyosarcoma.17 Notably, desmin has also been reported in other soft-tissue tumors including aggressive angiomyxoma, Ewing's Sarcoma, and in rare instances, CAF.17 There is also variability in ER and CD34 staining in CAF. CD34 stain is reportedly positive in approximately 60 % of CAF cases,8 while ER is positive in approximately 50 %.19Spindle Cell Lipoma (SCL) is closely related to CAF and is considered in the differential diagnosis. SCL presents as a slow growing mass and is treated with complete excision.8,10 Recurrence is reportedly rare.10 Microscopically, SCL is characterized by ropey eosinophilic collagen bundles, low-mitotic rates, bland spindle cells, adipocytes and mast cells.8,10 Immunohistochemistry analysis for SCL shows variable CD34, negative desmin, and variable S100.10 Genetically both SCL and CAF are characterized by loss in 13q14 chromosome, which contains the RB1 gene.10
RB1 encodes Retinoblastoma Protein (Rb), a potent tumor suppressor protein, which is hypothesized to play a role in tumorigenesis of SCL, CAF, and a variety of other cancers.16Morphologically both SCL and CAF contain spindle cells, however; CAF's characteristic hyalinized blood vessels may aid in a differential diagnosis in its favor. Since desmin is typically negative for SCL and has been reported in a minority of CAF cases, this may be an additional differentiating factor. Progesterone is also typically associated with CAF rather than SCL.10 Nucci et al. note that SCL is predominant to males and typically localizes to the cervical region.6Excision of paratesticular CAF has positive long term outcomes with low recurrence rates and no need for additional systemic therapy.6,8 Our case demonstrates the significance of the diagnostic process of paratesticular CAF, as immunohistochemistry staining may vary among paratesticular CAF. Further research is warranted to better understand the variable expression of desmin, CD34, and ER in paratesticular CAF to more accurately characterize CAF and aid in its distinction from other soft-tissue tumors. In addition, future investigation is needed to enhance the current understanding of estrogen's role in CAF pathogenesis.7

Conclusion

We describe a case of paratesticular CAF. Although the tumor contained morphological and immunological markers suggestive of CAF, our case of paratesticular CAF also demonstrated positivity for ER, CD34, and desmin stains. Positive desmin, CD34, and ER staining has been variably reported in paratesticular CAF.11,14 In order to enhance the understanding of CAF, future research is warranted to investigate paratesticular CAF pathogenesis and its immunohistochemistry markers.

Funding statement

This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

David Buchinsky: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Gaurav Pahouja: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest regarding the publication of this article.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 55-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Scrotal pain of two months duration
- **History of Present Illness:** Intermittent scrotal pain without specific exacerbating or relieving factors
- **Past Medical History:** Hypertension, migraines, neuropathy, nephrolithiasis, erectile dysfunction, and depression
- **Personal History:** Non-smoker, no history of alcohol use
- **Family History:** No family history of genitourinary malignancies
- **Physical Examination:** Abnormal midline scrotal mass; separate and normal palpable testicles; no hydrocele or varicocele; normal overlying skin.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Ultrasound:** Heterogeneous, hypoechoic midline scrotal mass (4.8 cm) with significant vascularity. Normal testicular appearance.
  - **CT Scan:** Midline scrotal mass (3.6 cm) with marked vascularity; no lymphadenopathy, bone lesions, or metastases.
  - **Histopathology:** Tumor histology revealed spindle cells, hyalinized vessels, wispy collagen fibers, mast cells, lymphocytic infiltration, and fibrous stroma. Immunohistochemical findings: Positive for CD34, desmin, and estrogen receptor (ER); negative for calretinin. Margins free of neoplasm.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential List**: Paratesticular masses considered included liposarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, angiomyofibroblastoma, aggressive angiomyxoma, inguinal hernia, hydrocele, spermatocele, and scrotal cyst.
   - **Rationale**: Based on clinical presentation of a painless scrotal mass and imaging findings; benign and malignant conditions were included for evaluation.
   
2. **Exclusion Steps Based on Findings**:
   - Malignant conditions (e.g., liposarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma): Less likely due to absence of metastatic disease or lymphadenopathy and benign histological features of the mass.
   - Inflammatory/infectious causes (e.g., infection, hydrocele): Excluded due to lack of relevant symptoms (e.g., systemic infection) or imaging evidence.
   - Angiomyofibroblastoma and aggressive angiomyxoma: Considered but excluded based on distinct histological features, including absence of their specific immunohistochemical markers.
   
3. **Closer Analysis Between Cellular Angiofibroma (CAF) and Spindle Cell Lipoma (SCL)**:
   - CAF favored due to characteristic hyalinized blood vessels, wispy collagen fibers, mast cells, and positive staining for CD34, desmin, and ER. These findings align more with CAF and distinguish it from SCL despite overlapping features (e.g., spindle-shaped cells).
   - SCL less likely due to typical absence of desmin staining in SCL and localization to the cervical region in most cases.

4. **Final Confirmation**:
   - Positive CD34, ER, and desmin staining combined with characteristic histological findings (e.g., hyalinized vessels, spindle cells) support the diagnosis of CAF over other paratesticular tumors.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **scrotal Cellular Angiofibroma (CAF)**. The diagnosis was supported by clinical presentation, imaging, surgical findings, and characteristic histopathological and immunohistochemical features.

- **Patient Information:** 55-year-old male

- **Past Medical History:** Hypertension, migraines, neuropathy, nephrolithiasis, erectile dysfunction, and depression

- **Physical Examination:** Abnormal midline scrotal mass; separate and normal palpable testicles; no hydrocele or varicocele; normal overlying skin.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Ultrasound:** Heterogeneous, hypoechoic midline scrotal mass (4.8 cm) with significant vascularity. Normal testicular appearance.
  - **CT Scan:** Midline scrotal mass (3.6 cm) with marked vascularity; no lymphadenopathy, bone lesions, or metastases.
  - **Histopathology:** Tumor histology revealed spindle cells, hyalinized vessels, wispy collagen fibers, mast cells, lymphocytic infiltration, and fibrous stroma. Immunohistochemical findings: Positive for CD34, desmin, and estrogen receptor (ER); negative for calretinin. Margins free of neoplasm.

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 55-year-old male with a two-month history of scrotal pain. The physical exam found a midline scrotal mass, separate from the testicles, no hydrocele or varicocele. The ultrasound and CT show a heterogeneous, hypoechoic mass with significant vascularity, and the histopathology shows spindle cells, hyalinized vessels, collagen fibers, mast cells, lymphocytes, and fibrous stroma. Immunohistochemistry is positive for CD34, desmin, estrogen receptor, and negative for calretinin. Margins are clear.

First, considering the location of the mass—midline scrotum. Common scrotal masses include testicular tumors, epididymal cysts, hernias, or infections. But the testes are normal on ultrasound, so it's not a testicular tumor. The mass is separate from the testicles, so maybe something else.

The imaging shows a vascular mass. Vascular tumors like hemangiomas or angiosarcomas? But the histology mentions spindle cells, which might suggest a sarcoma. However, the immunohistochemistry is positive for CD34 and desmin. CD34 is often seen in vascular tumors, and desmin is a marker for muscle cells. So maybe a vascular tumor with some spindle cell features. But also, estrogen receptor positive is unusual for typical vascular tumors. 

Wait, the histology mentions hyalinized vessels, which could be part of a hemangiopericytoma. Hemangiopericytomas are spindle cell tumors with a pericytic origin, often CD34 positive. They can be aggressive but sometimes benign. However, they usually have a more defined vascular pattern. Also, the presence of desmin might suggest some myogenic component, but that's not typical for hemangiopericytoma. 

Another possibility is a leiomyoma, but those are usually smooth muscle tumors and might not have the same CD34 positivity. The estrogen receptor positivity is more commonly seen in certain types of tumors, like some sarcomas or even gynecomastia-related issues, but here it's in the scrotum. 

What about a benign vascular tumor, like a hemangioma? But the size is 4.8 cm on ultrasound, which is quite large. Hemangiomas are usually more in children or younger adults. Also, the histology mentions spindle cells and hyalinized vessels, which might not fit a simple hemangioma. 

Another angle: the patient's past medical history includes hypertension, migraines, neuropathy, nephrolithiasis, erectile dysfunction, and depression. None of these directly point to a specific tumor, but maybe some systemic conditions? Not sure. The family history is negative for genitourinary malignancies, so hereditary factors are less likely.

The immunohistochemistry is key here. CD34 and desmin positive, ER positive, calretinin negative. CD34 is a marker for endothelial cells, so vascular tumors. Desmin is for smooth muscle. ER positivity is less common in vascular tumors. Maybe a variant of a hemangiopericytoma or a spindle cell tumor with vascular features. 

Alternatively, a solitary fibrous tumor (SFT) can be CD34 positive, but they are usually found in the pleura or other sites. However, they can occur in other locations. SFTs are spindle cell tumors with a CD34 positivity, and they can have a variable ER expression. But the presence of desmin might not fit here. 

Another thought: the tumor could be a myopericytoma, which is a variant of hemangiopericytoma with more myogenic features, hence desmin positivity. That might explain the desmin and CD34. ER positivity is less typical, but possible. 

The absence of lymphadenopathy or metastases on CT suggests a localized lesion. The margins are free of neoplasm, so it's likely a benign or low-grade malignant tumor. 

Putting it all together: the histology with spindle cells, vascular features, CD34 and desmin positivity, ER positive, and negative calretinin points towards a hemangiopericytoma or a myopericytoma. Since the margins are clear and no metastases, it's probably a benign or low-grade tumor. But the term "hemangiopericytoma" is often used, though some consider it a variant of a spindle cell tumor. 

Alternatively, the diagnosis could be a spindle cell hemangioma, but that's more common in the skin. Given the location and features, the most likely diagnosis is hemangiopericytoma, but with the desmin positivity, maybe a myopericytoma. However, the standard terminology might still use hemangiopericytoma. 

I think the answer here is hemangiopericytoma, given the CD34 positivity and vascular features, despite the desmin and ER. Or perhaps a spindle cell tumor with vascular differentiation. But the exact name might depend on the specific immunohistochemical profile. However, the presence of CD34 and desmin could be a variant. Alternatively, the ER positivity might be a red herring, or it could be a different entity. 

Wait, another possibility: a fibrohistiocytic tumor, but they usually don't have the CD34 and desmin. 

Alternatively, a metastatic tumor, but the CT shows no other lesions. The patient's history of nephrolithiasis and erectile dysfunction doesn't suggest a primary elsewhere. 

So, considering all, the most likely diagnosis is a hemangiopericytoma, or a related spindle cell tumor with vascular features. The final answer would be hemangiopericytoma, given the CD34 and vascular histology, even if there are some atypical markers like desmin and ER.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a midline scrotal mass, separate from the testicles, with no hydrocele or varicocele. Imaging reveals a vascular, heterogeneous mass, suggesting a non-testicular origin. The absence of lymphadenopathy or metastases on CT supports a localized lesion.

<step 2> Histopathology shows spindle cells, hyalinized vessels, and fibrous stroma, with CD34 and desmin positivity. CD34 is a marker for vascular endothelial cells, while desmin suggests myogenic differentiation. This combination is characteristic of hemangiopericytoma, a spindle cell tumor arising from pericytes surrounding blood vessels.

<step 3> Estrogen receptor (ER) positivity is atypical for classical hemangiopericytoma but may indicate a variant or a tumor with mixed features. Calretinin negativity excludes mesothelioma. The absence of calretinin and the presence of CD34, desmin, and ER align with a pericytic tumor rather than a mesothelial or other spindle cell lesion.

<step 4> The tumor's margins are free of neoplasm, and no metastases are present, supporting a localized, likely benign or low-grade malignant lesion. The combination of vascular histology, CD34 positivity, and desmin expression strongly points to hemangiopericytoma, despite the ER positivity, which may reflect tumor heterogeneity or a rare variant.

### Answer:
Hemangiopericytoma
```
